data.title: Commercial Airspace Over Venezuela to Reopen, Trump Says — WSJ
data.description:
By Vera Bergengruen, Ryan Dubé and Kejal Vyas

President Trump told Venezuelan interim leader Delcy Rodriguez that he will be opening all commercial airspace over the country.

"American citizens will be very shortly able to go to Venezuela, and they'll be safe there," Trump said Thursday at a cabinet meeting at the White House.

Trump said he ordered Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy and U.S. military officials to work on opening up the airspace by the end of the day, lifting restrictions that have barred U.S. commercial aircraft from operating over Venezuela.

Commercial air travel between the U.S. and Venezuela has been suspended since 2019, when diplomatic relations between the two countries broke down. The State Department still lists Venezuela at the highest risk level for travel, warning U.S. citizens not to travel to or remain in the country due to high risk of wrongful detention, torture, terrorism, kidnapping and civil unrest.

Trump on Thursday said the situation was improving and praised Rodriguez's leadership. "They have informed me they feel this very strong security," he said.

In November, Trump said the airspace surrounding Venezuela should be considered closed amid escalating tension and a military buildup that led to the Jan. 3 raid that captured Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro in Caracas.

The day of the operation, the Federal Aviation Administration banned U.S. commercial aircraft from operating in airspace above Venezuela and over that country's territorial waters.

Trump's announcement is the latest step in the administration's effort to normalize ties with Caracas. The State Department has formally notified Congress of plans to begin resuming operations in its U.S. Embassy in Caracas. On Thursday, Trump said major U.S. oil companies would travel to Venezuela to "scout out" locations for energy projects.

Rodriguez said this week her government was "unblocking" the country's frozen assets, which would be used to buy equipment for hospitals and for the electricity and gas sectors. On Thursday, Venezuela's congress was expected to approve changes to the hydrocarbons law that ease tight state control over its oil industry by providing private companies more autonomy to operate.

The reform will codify into law opaque oil deals known as productive participation contracts, which Maduro had implemented to circumvent U.S. sanctions on the oil industry. Companies will be able to sign contracts with state oil company Petróleos de Venezuela SA or its affiliates, allowing the private firms to operate state-owned oil fields.

The hydrocarbons bill keeps the maximum royalty payment that must be paid to the government, based on the volume of oil extracted, at 30%, but says the government can reduce the rate based on the economics of a project and other factors like capital investment requirements. U.S. and Venezuelan oil experts say the law will likely benefit companies already in Venezuela, but won't be enough to attract new foreign investments needed to revive the decrepit oil industry.

"It probably doesn't go far enough to attract sufficient investment, but it's a big step from where they were three weeks ago," Secretary of State Marco Rubio said Wednesday.

Since the operation that took down Maduro, the regime headed by Rodriguez, his former vice president, has largely maintained its repression tactics. Convoys of heavily armed and masked military intelligence officers patrol through the streets and set up roadblocks to inspect vehicles and often check passengers' phones.

Write to Vera Bergengruen at vera.bergengruen@wsj.com, Ryan Dubé at ryan.dube@wsj.com and Kejal Vyas at kejal.vyas@wsj.com

data.title: Utah Medical Products, Inc. Reports Year 2025 and Fourth Quarter Financial Performance
data.description:
Utah Medical Products, Inc. (UTMD), a company specializing in healthcare products for women and their babies, has released its financial results for the year 2025 and the fourth quarter ending December 31, 2025. Despite facing unexpected challenges, the company maintained strong profit margins and increased its year-end cash balances.

Financial Highlights

For the year 2025, UTMD reported a 5.8% decline in net sales, totaling $38.5 million compared to $40.9 million in 2024. Gross profit also saw a decrease of 8.9%, amounting to $22.0 million. Operating income dropped by 16.1% to $11.4 million, while income before tax fell by 16.0% to $14.1 million. Net income for the year was $11.3 million, an 18.7% decrease from the previous year. Earnings per share (EPS) were $3.483, down 12.1% from $3.961 in 2024.

Business and Operational Highlights

UTMD's revenue decline was primarily attributed to a significant drop in OEM sales to PendoTECH and lower sales to its China distributor. The company also experienced a decrease in worldwide Filshie device sales. Despite these challenges, domestic Filshie device sales increased by 10.8%, and the company saw a 5.9% rise in domestic direct sales of other devices.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

In 2025, UTMD repurchased 148,935 shares for $8.4 million and paid $4.0 million in dividends to stockholders. The company also invested $371,000 in new manufacturing equipment and tooling. UTMD's balance sheet remained strong, with cash and investments increasing to $85.8 million by year-end.

Management's Perspective

Management acknowledged the challenges faced in 2025, including unexpected order cancellations and increased costs due to inflation. However, they highlighted the company's ability to maintain strong profit margins and increase cash balances. The leadership also emphasized the importance of share repurchases and dividends in delivering value to stockholders.

Future Outlook

Looking ahead to 2026, UTMD expects OEM sales to PendoTECH and blood pressure monitoring kits to China to be zero. The company plans to offset these losses with new product sales and modest growth in its organic business, including domestic Filshie device sales and improvement in OUS Filshie device sales.

Original SEC Filing: UTAH MEDICAL PRODUCTS INC [ UTMD ] - 8-K - Jan. 29, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: NeOnc Technologies Signs Securities Purchase Agreement With Institutional Investor
data.description:
NeOnc Technologies entered into a Securities Purchase Agreement to sell up to 2,222,222 shares of common stock at $7.20 per share and issue five-year warrants to purchase up to 2,222,222 shares at $9.00 per share. An initial closing delivered 1,388,888 shares and matching warrants to a single institutional investor for $10 million, with additional closings expected. The company will file a resale registration statement within 10 days of the initial closing. Net proceeds will be used to repay debt and for working capital.

data.title: Argentina reduces taxes on conventional crude oil exports
data.description:
Argentina on Thursday cut export taxes on conventional oil to ease pressure on a sector struggling with high costs and depleted reserves.

The move comes after efforts to develop the Vaca Muerta shale formation, now the country's main source of fossil fuels.

• The government raised the floor price upon which export taxes are imposed on crude oil from $45 to $65 per barrel, a decree published in the country's Official Gazette said.

• Taxes will be calculated using a specific formula, reaching a maximum of 8% of export value when prices hit $80 per barrel.

• Conventional oil production faces challenges from depleted reserves, rising operational costs and international macroeconomic conditions, the government said in a decree.

data.title: Silicom Shares Up on Narrower Loss, Higher Revenue
data.description:
By Amira McKee

Shares of Silicom surged after the company narrowed its fourth-quarter loss on double-digit revenue growth.

The stock jumped 32% to $19.82 in Thursday afternoon trading, marking a 52-week high. The shares have risen 13% in the past year.

The data infrastructure company had a fourth-quarter loss of $2.53 million, or 44 cents per share, compared with a loss of $6.13 million, or $1.05 per share, in the year prior.

Adjusted loss was 34 cents per share.

Revenue rose 17% to $16.9 million.

Management expects revenue for the first quarter of 2026 will range from $16.5 million to $17.5 million, in line with the company's expectation of generating double digit-annual growth.

Write to Amira McKee at amira.mckee@wsj.com

data.title: Why Is the Market Down Today? — WSJ
data.description:
By WSJ Staff

Technology stocks are driving market benchmarks lower Thursday, but many sectors and stocks have been spared from the downturn.

  • Microsoft is the biggest reason U.S. stocks are down at the moment. The tech giant is one of four companies with a market cap above $3 trillion. That gives it big sway over the Nasdaq composite and the S&P 500. This afternoon, Microsoft is down 12% after investors raised worries about the company's cloud-business revenue. Microsoft's slump erased more than $400 billion of market value.
  • The Nasdaq was hit hard by the Microsoft decline and big drops in other software stocks such as Workday, Datadog and Atlassian. The tech-heavy index was down 1.3%. The S&P tech sector fell 2.8%.
  • The damage wasn't particularly widespread however. The Dow industrials were down only 0.2%, and 20 of the 30 stocks in the blue-chip average were recently up on the day. IBM, Honeywell and Merck were all up 2.5% or more, aiding the Dow.
  • A majority of S&P sectors are also up on the day. A rally in Meta Platforms has helped the communications services sector, while JPMorgan and other banks are boosting financial stocks.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: U.S. Natural Gas Futures Gain As Cold Weather to Continue — Market Talk
data.description: U.S. natural gas futures rise as the March contract moves to the front of the curve with enough cold weather ahead to keep up solid heating demand. "Frigid temperatures continue to drive very strong national demand through the weekend," NatGasWeather.com says in a note. Some production freeze-offs could continue, while LNG feedgas flows have recovered as southern U.S. temperatures warmed, the forecaster adds. "We expect much of the coming frosty pattern is accounted for and the natural gas markets are now focused on whether the Feb. 8-14 pattern proves cold enough across the northern U.S. or not." Nymex gas for March delivery settles up 5% at $3.918.mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Farmers Insurance Wants to Get Deeper Into Wealth Management. It's Launching an RIA. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Kenneth Corbin

Farmers Insurance is expanding its wealth management offerings with the launch of a new registered investment advisory arm, dubbed Farmers Asset Management. The RIA builds on Farmers' existing wealth offering, a broker-dealer closely tied to the company's insurance business.

Farmers bills the RIA as supporting a more holistic service model with the introduction of mainstays of the wealth management world such as comprehensive financial planning.

"We are building a full spectrum of financial offerings because investment management and insurance drive mutual growth and help customers with a broad array of financial needs," says Zachary Schear, president of Farmers Asset Management.

Schear has led the existing wealth business, Farmers Financial Services, a broker-dealer and insurance agency that oversees more than $10 billion in client assets, since January 2024. FFS, which has been in operation for more than 25 years, has a field staff of more than 2,500 registered representatives across the country.

Farmers' deeper foray into wealth management comes as many expanding RIAs have been looking to widen their own menu of services, developing either in-house or outsourced expertise in areas such as accounting, estate planning, and insurance.

The new RIA will rely on Charles Schwab for custody, clearing, and other services. It has tapped Orion for services such as technology and outsourced chief investment officer support.

The RIA will offer Farmers' insurance customers "a trusted avenue for investment advice, financial planning, and asset management," says Ken Walton, president of distribution at Farmers Insurance, who describes the new offering as retaining a strong focus on Farmers' core insurance business.

"We believe this expanded wealth management platform will attract growth-oriented, fee-based advisors who are ready to elevate their businesses by providing more comprehensive services and appreciate the important role that risk, guaranteed income, and insurance products can play in helping clients reach their financial goals," he says.

Write to advisor.editors@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: These Stocks Are Today's Movers: Microsoft, Meta, Tesla, Joby Aviation, SAP, Royal Caribbean, and More — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Mackenzie Tatananni, George Glover, and Joe Woelfel

Stocks fell Thursday as investors waded through a flurry of Big Tech earnings and a drop in Microsoft shares pulled the Nasdaq Composite down 1.5%.

These stocks were making moves:

Microsoft tumbled 12% even as fiscal second-quarter earnings and revenue beat analysts' expectations. Investors are worried about Microsoft's cloud division: Revenue for the Azure cloud computing platform rose 39% from a year earlier, down slightly from 40% growth in the first quarter. Chief Financial Officer Amy Hood said on a conference call that Microsoft expects third-quarter Azure revenue growth of between 37% and 38% in constant currency. Wall Street was expecting 37.6%.

Meta Platforms jumped 9.5%. The parent company of Facebook and Instagram posted fourth-quarter earnings and revenue that beat Wall Street estimates. Meta also issued first-quarter revenue guidance that was better than expected and said capital spending could reach up to $135 billion in 2026, about 20% higher than Wall Street expectations and nearly double from a year earlier.

Tesla stock fell 1.8% after rising earlier in the session. The electric-vehicle maker's fourth-quarter earnings and revenue beat Wall Street forecasts. Tesla said it would invest $2 billion to buy Series E preferred shares in xAI, CEO Elon Musk's artificial-intelligence start-up. The company also said it would discontinue production of its higher-end Model X and Model S vehicles, and planned to launch a robo-taxi service in the first half of 2026 in seven new cities.

International Business Machines climbed 6.3%. IBM beat analysts' targets for fourth-quarter profit and revenue as sales in the company's software segment rose 14% to a stronger-than-expected $9 billion. Separately, the company unveiled the latest results of a quantum computing experiment.

ServiceNow slumped 11%. Although earnings and revenue for the fourth quarter were better than anticipated, investors likely were fretting about a slew of deals the enterprise software company has made as it looks to better position itself in AI.

Lam Research advanced 1.3%. The maker of chip manufacturing tools beat analyst estimates for fiscal second-quarter adjusted earnings and revenue. As the AI frenzy accelerates, Lam "ramping execution velocity across the company to support our customers' growth," CEO Tim Archer said.

Las Vegas Sands sank 14%. The casino and resorts company reported adjusted earnings for the key Macao market that fell short of analysts' expectations, partly due to higher events and payroll costs.

U.S.-listed shares of SAP plunged 15%. The German software company topped earnings estimates but missed on revenue for the fourth quarter, with cloud revenue also falling short of expectations.

Royal Caribbean Group soared 14%. While adjusted earnings and revenue for the fourth quarter were in line with expectations, the cruise operator's 2026 earnings guidance blew past forecasts.

Southwest Airlines surged 16% and was the top stock in the S&P 500 Thursday after the carrier reported fourth-quarter adjusted earnings that beat Wall Street forecasts and projected a big jump in 2026 profit. Southwest said it was benefiting from revenue initiatives, including charging for seat assignments and extra-legroom seats, along with the checked-bag fees, and continued cost controls.

L3Harris Technologies fell 2%. The defense contractor topped analysts' estimates for fourth-quarter adjusted earnings but missed on revenue. Full-year guidance was soft.

Honeywell International rose 4.5%. The industrial conglomerate reported fourth-quarter adjusted earnings of $2.59 a share on adjusted sales of $10.1 billion. Wall Street had forecast earnings of $2.54 a share on sales of $10 billion.

Apple ticked up slighty. The iPhone maker is set to report its fiscal first-quarter earnings after Thursday's closing bell. Investors will be watching iPhone sales, since that's where Apple makes most of its revenue, as well as memory costs, which have surged due to the rise in demand for AI.

Altria Group was down 3.3%. The maker of Marlboro cigarettes reported weaker-than-expected quarterly earnings. Shipment volumes in Altria's oral tobacco unit decreased 6.3%, driven by a loss of market share across its product line.

Write to Mackenzie Tatananni at mackenzie.tatananni@barrons.com and George Glover at george.glover@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Latam FX edges higher buoyed by commodities strength; geopolitics in focus
data.description:
Key points:
  • Chile's peso leads Latam FX higher, tracking higher copper prices
  • Brazil's real slightly up after cenbank leaves rates steady
  • Geopolitics in focus after US threatens attack on Iran

By Purvi Agarwal and Johann M Cherian

Most currencies of resources-rich Latin American economies were flat to slightly higher on Thursday, tracking higher commodity prices, while investors also took stock of geopolitical tensions after the United States threatened to attack Iran.

MSCI's index tracking Latam currencies against the dollar (.MILA00000CUS) edged up 0.2%, and was set for its ninth session in positive territory.

The Fed kept rates unchanged as expected on Wednesday, with Chair Jerome Powell describing a solid economy and diminished risks to inflation and employment, which could signal a lengthy wait before any further cuts in borrowing costs.

Still, Powell's mildly hawkish comments provided little support to the dollar, which eased against most currencies.

COPPER PRICE CLIMB LIFTS CHILE'S PESO

Chile's peso gained the most among peers, up 0.8% to its highest level in more than two years. The rise in the currency of the world's largest copper producer was tracking higher prices of the red metal, where futures on the London Metal Exchange crossed $14,000 a metric ton.

Data showed the unemployment rate in Chile hit 8.0% in the quarter through December, below the 8.3% expected by economists polled by Reuters.

Brazil's real inched up 0.1% in choppy trading after the country's central bank on Wednesday held interest rates steady at a near two-decade-high of 15% but signaled it would start cutting at its next meeting in March, a move Finance Minister Fernando Haddad said will help put public debt on a better trajectory.

"The swaps curve implies 275bps of BCB rate cuts to 12.25% in the next twelve months. The risk is BCB does not ease as much as is currently priced in given that the October 2026 general election skews fiscal policy toward near-term spending support," said Elias Haddad, global head of markets strategy at Brown Brothers Harriman.

The country's equity market fell 0.8%, weighed down by financial stocks. The banking sector is grappling with the repercussions of fraud by liquidated Banco Master.

Brazilian state-run bank BRB <BSLI3.SA> will likely have to set aside more than 5 billion reais ($970 million) to cover transactions with failed lender Banco Master, a central bank director told the federal police in testimony seen by Reuters.

Mexico's peso was flat and Colombia's currency firmed 0.5%, supported by a 3% advance in oil prices after the U.S. threatened to attack Iran.

Investors were also on the lookout for any clues about the renegotiation of the USMCA treaty later this year. Against this backdrop, U.S. President Donald Trump and Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum spoke about trade and security, with both leaders saying that the call was productive.

The MSCI measure of Latam stocks (.MILA00000PUS) slipped 0.2%, but was still near its highest since early 2018. Stocks in EMs broadly have rallied to record highs recently as investors diversified out of U.S. stocks, and as the frenzy around AI-linked and commodity-heavy equities helped lift appetite.

On the trade front, El Salvador said it signed a trade agreement with the United States, although details were scant.

Ecuador's Economy Minister Sariha Moya said the country will save $416 million in debt payments this year after a successful debt-management operation launched earlier this week. The country's international bonds were marginally lower after a rally earlier this week.

data.title: NewEdge Advisors Recruits $1.4 Billion Team From Ameriprise — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Kenneth Corbin

NewEdge Advisors has recruited a large team away from Ameriprise, where the 24-person practice managed $1.4 billion in client assets. The addition of ClearTrust Advisors, based in Franklin, Tenn., strengthens NewEdge's presence in the Nashville market.

ClearTrust is led by Joe Creecy, Matt Robins, and Nick Stamatis, who each have the titles of private wealth advisor and managing partner. The practice offers financial planning and holistic wealth management services for a client base that includes small business entrepreneurs, corporate executives, and multigenerational families. Its team includes 12 advisors and 12 support staffers.

ClearTrust represents "exactly the type of advisor-led organization we are proud to support," says Alex Goss, CEO of NewEdge Advisors. "Their commitment to client service, thoughtful growth, and team culture aligns closely with our mission."

A NewEdge spokeswoman says the firm has notched other recruiting wins this year, but the ClearTrust transition is the first one it has announced widely. "Our pipeline remains strong coming off 2025, our largest recruiting year in our history," she says.

NewEdge Advisors is a division of NewEdge Capital Group, a Pittsburgh-based firm that caters to wealthy clients, including ultrahigh-net-worth families. It was ranked No. 3 on Barron's 2025 list of top RIA firms. NewEdge says it oversaw more than $88 billion in client assets as of Dec. 31.

ClearTrust has a veteran leadership team. Creecy entered the wealth management industry with Ameriprise in 2000. Robins first registered with Ameriprise in 2003, and Stamatis joined the Minneapolis firm in 2005. Registration records indicate the team formally joined NewEdge on Jan. 23.

An Ameriprise spokeswoman didn't immediately respond to a request for comment on the departure.

ClearTrust opted to join NewEdge "to gain the flexibility and strategic support needed to advance our long-term vision," Creecy says. "The partnership allows us to enhance the client experience without compromising the values and relationships at the core of our practice, while positioning the firm for continued growth."

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Front Month Nymex ULSD Fell 3.03% to Settle at $2.5854 — Data Talk
data.description:
Front Month Nymex ULSD for Feb. delivery lost 8.07 cents per gallon, or 3.03% to $2.5854 today

  • Largest one day dollar decline since Thursday, Nov. 20, 2025
  • Largest one day percentage decline since Thursday, Jan. 15, 2026
  • Snaps a four session winning streak
  • Today's settlement value is the third highest this year
  • Off 4.28% from its 52-week high of $2.7011 hit Tuesday, Nov. 18, 2025
  • Up 30.94% from its 52-week low of $1.9745 hit Monday, May 5, 2025
  • Rose 4.44% from 52 weeks ago
  • Off 3.03% from its 2026 settlement high of $2.6661 hit Wednesday, Jan. 28, 2026
  • Up 25.71% from its 2026 settlement low of $2.0567 hit Wednesday, Jan. 7, 2026
  • Off 49.66% from its record high of $5.1354 hit Thursday, April 28, 2022
  • Month-to-date it is up 21.92%
  • Year-to-date it is up 46.48 cents or 21.92%

All prices are calculated based on the settlement price of the current front month contract.

Source: Dow Jones Market Data

data.title: Front Month Nymex RBOB Gasoline Rose 1.46% to Settle at $1.9200 — Data Talk
data.description:
Front Month Nymex RBOB Gasoline for Feb. delivery gained 2.77 cents per gallon, or 1.46% to $1.9200 today

  • Up for three consecutive sessions
  • Up 9.99 cents or 5.49% over the last three sessions
  • Largest three day dollar gain since Wednesday, July 30, 2025
  • Largest three day percentage gain since Monday, Jan. 12, 2026
  • Up 12 of the past 15 sessions
  • Highest settlement value since Wednesday, Nov. 19, 2025
  • Off 17.63% from its 52-week high of $2.331 hit Wednesday, April 2, 2025
  • Up 14.22% from its 52-week low of $1.6809 hit Tuesday, Dec. 16, 2025
  • Down 5.76% from 52 weeks ago
  • Up 13.31% from its 2026 settlement low of $1.6945 hit Wednesday, Jan. 7, 2026
  • Off 55.10% from its record high of $4.2762 hit Thursday, June 9, 2022
  • Month-to-date it is up 12.58%
  • Year-to-date it is up 21.46 cents or 12.58%

All prices are calculated based on the settlement price of the current front month contract.

Source: Dow Jones Market Data

data.title: Carney Calls on White House to Respect Canadian Sovereignty Over Alberta
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney called on the Trump administration to respect his country's sovereignty after a group advocating for Alberta's secession from Canada said it has met with State Department officials about the province's future.

Last week, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent weighed on the province's political future, saying he was aware of a bid by some citizens to hold a referendum on independence and added that the oil-rich western Canadian province would be "a natural partner" for the U.S.

The future of Alberta marks the latest source of tension between Washington and Ottawa, triggered this month by Carney's trade pact with China, America's greatest rival; and a speech he delivered that marked a call-to-arms among smaller powers to collectively counter economic coercion from global hegemons keen to use their economic and military might to achieve policy goals.

"We expect the U.S. administration to respect Canadian sovereignty," Carney said Thursday, alongside the country's 10 provincial premiers, including Alberta's Danielle Smith. "I'm always clear in my conversation with President Trump to that effect."

A State Department official said the department meets regularly with civil-society groups, without confirming specific conversations with members of the Stay Free Alberta group. "As is typical in routine meetings such as these, no commitments were made," the official said, without providing further details.

Jeffrey Rath, legal counsel for the Stay Free Alberta movement, said his takeaway from the State Department is that the Trump administration "is very, very enthusiastic about the prospect of a free and independent Alberta." He said the talks with State Department officials have focused on "what a new relationship could look like."

Bessent said last week that Alberta's previous efforts to build a new oil-carrying pipeline to the Pacific Coast have faced opposition from Canadian officials, and suggested the province should consider building that pipeline through the U.S.

"Albertans are very independent people," Bessent told the Real America's Voice media outlet. "People want sovereignty. They want what the U.S. has got."

Rath said he interpreted Bessent's remarks as "to be tacitly in favor of Alberta independence, even though it wasn't clearly stated in that manner--but, certainly, 'wink, wink, nudge, nudge.'"

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com
